author: @tonyhardy-kp4os
text:
It is not due to the nature of water, it is due to the nature of the universe that the water heats up faster than it releases it. But it is only because you are putting a pot of water on a stove where the temperature differential is high, for example a propane flame is like 3,600F compared to the water at room temp of around 75F.  That is a starting differential of 3,525F so it will heat faster than it releases it because when you remove it from the flame after it reached 212F and leave it in the room of 75F so this differential is only 137F so it releases it slower then it took to heat it. But if i took it off the flame and put it in an absolute zero environment for example, then it would lose heat faster because the differential is higher. For example: a propane flame is 1982Celcis and absolute zero is -273.15Celcius, if i start out with water(ice) at -273.15C on a flame of 1982C(propane) is a differential of 2,255.15C, and when i remove from flame after it reaches 100C(boiling point of water at sea level) it will be in the room of -273.15C where the differential is now 173.15C(343.72F) but higher than our first example of only 137F(58.33C) between boiling temp of the water and the room in a room of 75F(23.88C), so it will dissipate the heat faster in a colder surrounding. But it is still a lower differential than when we had it on the flame so it still will heat faster but only because it is not cold enough in the room to cool it as fast. It is about the temp differential. 

This applies to all materials, that is why sand will do the same thing. Heat room temp sand with a propane flame and it will indeed heat faster than it releases it for the same reason water does it. Water is more dense but as you mentioned water boils at only 212F so sand holds more energy than water because you can go much higher in temp. But it will still have a lower differential once removed from the flame than with the flame same as the water, hence why it heats faster with the flame than it cools after removed from the flame. Example: Room temp sand heated to 1000F and removed from flame sitting in a room of 75F is still a lower differential than the 3,600F vs 75F we used to heat it, so it will release slower than it took to heat it and this is why. But it will release it faster in a -273C room than in a room of 23.88C(75F).

But one more thing, if i used a single candle to heat the water it takes longer to heat it than a flame burner from a propane stove and now if i remove the candle it could possible cool just as fast as it took to heat it if i put it out in negative temps because one candle is not many joules of energy compared to a propane flame off a stove. Plus i absolutely didn't express this correctly, i should have used joules as the measurement of energy not temperature but it still holds true that its dissipation speed is depending on the surrounding temps, in both directions heating or cooling right?

author: @barrybatchelor44
text: If you are using exhaust gases to heat the material I think you would be better to swap out the sand for round(ish) river stones and run your gases directly through a vessel of stones, you could then use piped oil or the same vents to move the heat energy to where you want it hooked to fan/pump to control heat via a thermostat. Most of that thermal heat will just be lost up that flue. If you really wanted to get smart run your gases into a cyclonic thermal oxidizer and work out a way to exchange that heat. I used to build commercial biomass gasifiers and once I get my new home workshop I'll be developing something like this. Rocket stoves are ok but I feel a tlud gasifier running on wood chip would work better with stone on a smaller scale.

author: @removechan10298
text:
NASA patent is one thing, but sand as a heat store has been used from 1500 onwards at LEAST, where coffee was produced on top of a large disc of sand over metal heated with fire underneath, the sand would act as a thermal store so they could keep topping up the coffee pots and keep pouring coffee in small cups, without having to continuously stoke or maintain the fires, and without directly tarnishing the pots in the fire, and with a more adjustable heat (they move it around in the sand so you have time to brew the coffee at the right speed)
Love your channels!

author: @Ma_X64
text: Heat is not a very good way to store energy (unless you need exactly the thermal energy), since all thermal converters (internal combustion engines, Peltier elements, Stirling engines) have low efficiency, which, moreover, drops even lower at the slightest deviation of the system parameters from the conditionally ideal. Perhaps gravitational accumulation is best suited, i.e. raising a certain working mass to a certain height. Often this is pumping water from bottom to top, but it can also be lifting a solid, heavy load. Such potential energy can be much more simply and efficiently converted into electrical energy using a conventional generator. And the efficiency of electric machines is rarely below 70%.

text:
Please make a Rocket Mass Heater Sand Battery on a four-wheel dolly that can, when moved, pass through a standard outside door.

When it threatens to get very cold out overnight, we can fire up the rocket mass heater in the early evening and when it burns out, we can pull out the embers from the burn chamber, cap the rocket heater ports (wood feed, cleanout, chimney) to keep the heat in, then roll the entire apparatus on the dolly wheels into the home to radiate heat slowly until morning. (or uncap the rocket heater ports in the home releasing the energy more quickly.  That would be super useful.

With it permanently on a four-wheel dolly you could go with a larger sand battery.

author: @poorman-trending
text: Not to get too technical, but with A/C there is also reactance that comes into play and potential for the radiative resistance of the wire to turn some of the input energy  into radio waves.  In an antenna this is good but something like this you want the radiation resistance low and the loss resistance high. At 50 or 60 hz it doesn’t have a huge effect as it does at higher frequencies but it is something that can be optimised for in the design of the heating element.

author: @michaelboyle4231
text: The Korean used underfloor sand battery to heat their homes for thousands of years known as ondol and some still use this method to this very day using sand and sandstone and clay floors with channels of clay bricks and very small fire at one end just below ground level and a chimney at other end of house. The hot air from the fire flows along clay brick channels with layers of sand on the bottom and sandstone and clay floor layout across the clay brick channels the hot air heating up the sand and bricks and sandstone and clay floor. When fire went out the heat would still be flowing from the underground floor channels up through the floor for up to 8 hours afterwards. These homes known as HANOK/ ondol homes.

author: @davefroman4700
text: The easiest sand battery you can make? Is with an old electric oven. All you need to ensure is that the bottom element is still functioning. Put 3-4 solar panels wired in series for 750-800 watts and then just take the positive and negative from either side of the array and hook that to the bottom element in the stove directly. Use some self tapping screws and washers to close the front up permanently. Remove the top element that has the oven vent under it, and fill the oven with dry sand.

author: @The_Real_Phil_Lee
text:
One great advantage of using a sand battery to store the heat is that it makes a great insulator for some of the very high-efficiency, very safe, electrical batteries (like liquid sodium or sodium-sulphur) which have far greater (for some, like lead or magnesium-antimony, infinite) recharge cycles but need very high temperatures to work.  Putting such batteries into a large volume of sand allows for the heat to be stored in the sand and electricity to be stored in the electrical portion in the middle of the sand, meaning no inefficient conversion is needed to turn stored heat into electricity for all the peripheral uses like communications, refrigeration, entertainment, computing, lighting and home automation.  The best of these technologies outperform pumped water storage in both efficiency and immediacy of response to demand.
As for resistance wire, if the right ones are chosen, the temperature coefficient of resistance will increase the resistance enough with temperature to avoid local overheating and concentrate the heat into the coolest parts of the sand.

text: It's worth noting that while sand is inexpensive in a simple sense, the monstrous sand mining industry is fraught with global implications. author: @sonofeloah
text: I have already been working on this type of system. But, being on a fixed income, resources need to be of recycled materials that not only cut the cost of building the unit, but alsi means that you are better likelyhood to find replacement naterials to recycle as opposed to new items that you buy and say a prolonged grid down either due to natural causes or a man made economic collapse. So, as I've already been using "sand" mixing into cement to make concrete, I am now storing up a resource that I can get plenty of "sand" from. Folks might be wondering why I have my sand in quotation and that is because it is recycled from a source that sand was used to make that resource. Glass. Crushed glass is sand if crushed into small enough granules. And anyu broken glass I come upon, I collect up and store away for future use. Yes, a proper heat storage is large for the home and greenhouses and requires a lot of sand. Yet glass is thrown away on a daily basis and is easy to collect and store. Now and then crush it up and store it in a bin of some sort so it takes up less space. If you are mindful about it, then it is easy to gather what you need and more. Remember, to buy sand is going up as fuel/transportation costs are going up. It costs me nothing to bring home some broken glass someone is or has discarded. After all, if it is on my way to wherever I am going anyway, then the cost is in the going to and from where I need to go and thus for a minute of my time to pick it up, it is otherwise free.

author: @Squire_Chris
text:
Just one person mentioned old hot water cylinders.  I'm thinking (for the money-conscious DiYer):
* Get an old cylinder off your local internet market place.  People are still getting rid of old units, some even have copper inners!  Sometimes available for free, just to get it off the owner's hands.
* Get an old vehicle radiator from a wrecker, install into the middle of the cylinder before filling with sand
* Alternatively, people are always throwing out cheap oil radiator/heaters, some are quite small

* Cylinders already come with very good insulation AND many of the better ones have an insulated hole designed for a heating element to be inserted and replaced, with a view to preventing electrolytic degeneration of an element immersed in water.  
With one of these you could experiment indefinitely with different resistive elements, different wattages, different resistances to suit your solar or other electrical energy you are wanting to store.  

* The radiator (either vehicular or heater-type) already has fins designed for effective heat-exchange.  
If still 100% liquid-proof, it would provide a secondary option for pumping heat INTO the sand-battery (say hot oil heat-exchange from another source).
At the very least, a small computer-fan could force air through the radiator (surrounded by hot sand) at whatever speed desired (perhaps even run from a Peltier device if you wish).  Any small leaks in the radiator certainly wouldn't affect its use as an air-driven heat exchange.

There is a better material ferrulite ie basically iron oxide. Get some scrap eg nails from burning pallets. Toss them in a bucket and add water and salt. They will rust so wash and fiter mix with refractory cement and form into whatever you like. Now the issue of the Stirling  they are crap. Build a flame gulper engine and feed it hot air heated from the sand battery.

author: @BrookDrummpb
text: Have you considered one or more fans on the exhaust pipe to scavenge the airflow for electricity? Many of your videos are singular ideas. Have you thought about combining several ideas into one machine? Like sand battery, wind, solar, battery bank, water heater, rocket stove, radiant heating, water heater… fresnel lens, steam, generator, sterling, oven for cooking. An open source multi-prong approach is largely achievable for diy. A smoke microcontroller could monitor the most efficient combo for weather conditions and seasonal loads. It could track usage as well to adapt for energy capture. This more complex vision could be approached in logical stages and slowly build upon itself. The efficiency of any single approach would find an overall median efficiency, and provide redundancy. I’m always baffled by the silver bullet approach- seeking the one ring to rule them all. While a broader approach may unlock a wider adoption- works anywhere , just add or subtract various elements for your region.

author: @kadmow
text:
Sang's ability to insulate, due to terrible thermal flow rate, is also a great property - adding a water coil into the sand battery gives a useful source of flash steam (low grade) for exchange with the environment.

Using an adequate sizing to maintain envelope temperatures to "manageable" increases usefulmess - don't worry, Austrian /Bavarian mass heaters were there already...
Thanks to geopolitics and social hysteria, we are in the "forever crisis" - and markets will expand to infinity (or so it seems)...

Australia has lots of iron ore - haematite - of various grades (geared to mine and export at scale),  which can have a life as sand battery media - and can easily be converted to iron after it is no longer needed.

text: I want to create a space heater for my shed that will be formed from a clip-lid 44 gallon drum filled with sand that will sit on a pallet to better distribute the weight (and keep the heat off my wood floor. I'll feed it with a solar panel and I like the idea of using a kettle element as the heater. I'll set the element in the bottom of the drum. I am just wondering how to go about the power feed. I'm thinking of setting a metal flange in the middle of the drum base to accept the element but could use some suggestions how I could make the electrical connection without shorting or melting the insulation. Is a Plaster of Paris plug a way to go or is there something better (cement perhaps)?  I will of course have the unit temperature controlled. I have no need for hundreds of degrees but something say 40C-ish would be dandy on a cold winter day.

replies.author: @justinw1765
replies.text:
If you got enough sun to run solar panels, it is actually more efficient to convert that Solar energy directly into heat.  And you can  build larger structures outside.  Personally, if my yard/property got plenty of sun, I would build two solar heat collectors--an immediate heater, and then one with a "battery" (sand, paraffin oil or wax, etc), so that when night came, would still get some warmth.  Just pipe the hot hair in through windows.  

   I haven't done this, simply because my property has too many trees/too much shade.  The few areas that get a decent amount of sun was too far away from the house.

author: @SundogbuildersNet
text:
Oh boy.. as a long time Rocket Mass Heater builder, ive got a lot to cringe about here.

First, very cool! I always appreciate your work . Now, the 1-2-4 proportion is an ideal that "always works" but the proportions can be fudged for reasons. The full explanation is long and full of qualifiers. ;)

One of my main objections to how you put this together is that you're taking heat directly from the combustion process. This lowers the temperature inside the combustion unit, reducing its efficiency by a large margin. Best practice is to take heat from AFTER the combustion process, I.E. after the riser. Of course this will make a much taller device but there is a workaround. 

My second objection: inside a well running RS, temperatures can rise to 1000° C, which in combination with excess O2 will burn the carbon out of your steel rather quickly. What you'll get is a pile of shards that was once a steel combustion unit. Those little 4 inch cookers are fine if they're NOT insulated or (in this case) surrounded in sand. Best practices (again), use refractory material for the combustion unit, whenever possible.

author: @awesomedee5421
text: I've never heard of that 1-2-4 ratio for rocket stove. I've built a bunch and I think the important aspect is the 4 or longer is better, and that the dia opening is the same as dia flue. I think if you built a rocket stove with a different ratio like 2-1-4 or something would see equal performance.

replies.author: @SundogbuildersNet
replies.text: The 1/2/4 ratio is considered t to be ideal and it is. Having said that, it's not an absolute. The horizontal, "burn tunnel" can be shorter without consequence but not a lot longer. If you're not drafting down to a bench or something, then the riser can be shorter.

replies.author: @SundogbuildersNet
replies.text: A higher feed creates, effectively,  a shorter riser. The difference in height is what's important.

author: @goldcountryruss7035
text: If you use round river rocks instead of sand, you will not need a heat exchange device to harvest the heat. Simply blow air in and remove hot air as necessary. The round rocks will store the same amount of heat per pound as sand, but it enables air flow with minimal back pressure.

replies.author: @Spoods117
replies.text: Good idea, somewhat lower density, I'd make a "screen" and test in and out airflow temps and attach to the side of a sand battery, pulling heat and working as intended

replies.author: @SundogbuildersNet
replies.text: Heat transfer to the medium will also be slower. Less surface area to the heat exchanger on account of the spaces between the gravel. This is also true with sand, where little clay can be added to fill the spaces and bond them together in a chunk.

replies.author: @jprice1122
replies.text: River rock “can” be dangerous to heat up.  If they have somehow retained any water in the matrix the water will expand and blow the rock apart.   Rare but it can happen.

author: @judyofthewoods
text: Wouldn't clay subsoil be a good substitute for sand? It could be dried to be used in freestanding batteries or, if part of a building, dug up and replaced after waterproof tanking was put into the pit with some heat-proof insulation between it and the clay. The clay can dry out in situ.

replies.author: @SundogbuildersNet
replies.text: Generally speaking, the heavier the material, the higher its thermal mass. Dug soil tends to be less mass than sand but it will work. You can wet that clay soil and pack it tight to improve mass. Add sand to it and it will become sculptable (also will increase mass) essentially cob but without the straw.

replies.author: @kerrryschultz2904
replies.text: Why not create longer risers for the rocket stove chimney, feed tube, and air inlet. Connect the chimney rise to a proper chimney and bury the whole works deep in the sand. The rocket stove would burn very clean because of the fire heat air triangle. After a burn you should then cap or restrict the air so it doesn't syphon all the heat up the chimney. In the 1970's they would build insulated boxes or sometimes use the entire foundation area to receive heat. In the case of the insulated box which would be in the basement, an insulated    lid would be cracked just enough to allow heat to escape. And by adjusting the opening would adjust for the heat required.
